1: Daniel 7-12 contains four major apocalyptic visions. The genre of apocalypse (from Greek apokalypsis, unveiling) is characterized by: symbolic or heavenly visions mediated by an angel, disclosure of the heavenly perspective on historical events, periodization of history into fixed sequences, and imminent divine intervention. Daniel is the OT's primary apocalyptic text; its imagery (beasts from the sea, the Ancient of Days, the Son of Man, the four kingdoms) was enormously influential on Jewish and Christian apocalyptic (1 Enoch, 4 Ezra, 2 Baruch, and the NT's Revelation). Jesus's eschatological discourse (Mark 13 and parallels) draws extensively from Daniel, particularly the abomination of desolation (Daniel 11:31; Daniel 12:11 → Mark 13:14) and the coming of the Son of Man (Daniel 7:13 → Mark 13:26).
2: The four winds of heaven (ʿarbaʿ ruchey shemayya) stirring up the great sea: the great sea (yamma rabba) is both the Mediterranean Sea (the geographic horizon of ANE maps) and the primordial chaos-sea of ANE cosmology. In Ugaritic and Babylonian mythology, the sea is the domain of Yam/Tiamat — the chaos-force that divine order must overcome. Daniel's vision opens with cosmic imagery that his ancient audience would have immediately associated with the primal struggle between divine order and cosmic chaos. The four winds suggest YHWH's sovereign control over what appears to be chaos: the beasts emerge from the stirred sea by divine permission, not random chance.
3: The four great beasts (arbaʿ khayvan rabbavan) arise from the sea — from chaos. The beast-from-the-sea image is the ANE symbol for human empires viewed from the heavenly perspective: great powers that appear to human observers as dominant forces are, from above, chaos-beasts permitted temporarily by the God who rules the sea. The contrast with Daniel 2 is significant: Daniel 2 presents the four empires as a magnificent statue viewed from a human perspective (gold, silver, bronze, iron); Daniel 7 presents the same four empires as terrifying beasts viewed from the divine perspective. Both interpretive frameworks are true simultaneously.
4: The first beast — a lion with eagle's wings — is the symbol of Babylon. Winged lions (lamassu) are ubiquitous in Babylonian and Assyrian palace art; the combination of the most powerful land animal and the most powerful bird was a standard ANE symbol of royal power. Nebuchadnezzar's own inscriptions describe him in lion imagery. The humiliation described — wings plucked, made to stand as a man, given a human heart — corresponds to the narrative of Nebuchadnezzar's madness and restoration (Daniel 4). The first beast is not just a symbol of Babylon in general but of Nebuchadnezzar specifically and the transformation he underwent.
5: The second beast — a bear raised on one side with three ribs in its mouth — represents Medo-Persia. The bear raised on one side may indicate the dominance of Persia over Media within the dual empire, or the two horns of the ram in Daniel 8. The three ribs (bones/meat) in its mouth are widely interpreted as three major conquests: Lydia (547 BCE), Babylon (539 BCE), and Egypt (525 BCE). The command “arise, devour much flesh” corresponds to the Persian empire's insatiable territorial expansion, reaching from India to Egypt at its height.
6: The third beast — a leopard with four bird-wings on its back and four heads — represents Greece, specifically Alexander the Great and his successors. The leopard's speed corresponds to the historically unprecedented speed of Alexander's conquest: from Greece to India in under thirteen years (334-323 BCE). Four wings on a four-headed beast specifically anticipates the breakup of Alexander's empire into four successor kingdoms after his death in 323 BCE: the Ptolemaic (Egypt), Seleucid (Syria/East), Antigonid (Macedonia), and Attalid/Lysimachid (Asia Minor) kingdoms. The correspondence is historically precise.
7: The fourth beast — dreadful, terrifying, exceedingly strong, with iron teeth and bronze claws, different from all the others — is Rome in the most common interpretation. The beast is deliberately left without an animal comparison: no natural creature is adequate to symbolize it. Its iron teeth connect to the iron legs and feet of Daniel 2. The ten horns correspond to the ten toes of Daniel 2's image. The historical identification with Rome is supported by the NT's application of Daniel's imagery to Rome in Revelation 13 and 17. Some scholars see the fourth beast as Greece and the little horn as Antiochus IV Epiphanes — this is the critical consensus but conflicts with the NT's future-oriented application.
8: The little horn (qeren zeira, small horn) that arises among the ten and uproots three: this figure has been identified with Antiochus IV Epiphanes (175-164 BCE), who is the explicit subject of Daniel 8 and 11. Antiochus IV arose from the Seleucid line, displaced three kings (Heliodorus, his nephew Demetrius, and Ptolemy VI), and conducted himself with “a mouth speaking great things” — his coins bore the inscription theos epiphanes (god manifest), the ultimate self-aggrandizing claim. The NT reapplies the little-horn figure to a future eschatological ruler (2 Thessalonians 2:3-4; Revelation 13:5-6), treating Antiochus as a type.
9: The Ancient of Days (ʿattiq yomaya, literally “advanced in days”) enthroned: the heavenly court scene. The title ʿattiq yomaya is unique to Daniel in the OT. The description — white garments, white hair, fiery throne, river of fire — draws on the standard ANE and Israelite imagery of divine majesty (cf. Exodus 24:10; Ezekiel 1:26-28). The thousands who serve him and the ten thousands who stand before him constitute the divine council or heavenly assembly (sod) attested throughout the OT (Psalms 82:1; Isaiah 6:1-3; 1 Kings 22:19-22). Judgment is about to be given: the court is seated and books are opened.
10: The river of fire (nahar di-nur, river of fire) proceeding from before YHWH: fire is the standard biblical symbol of divine holiness that destroys what is impure (Deuteronomy 4:24; Hebrews 12:29). The river proceeding — not static but flowing — suggests ongoing divine judgment-activity. The ten thousand times ten thousand who stand before him: the census of the heavenly host. In the ANE, the heavenly court functioned as the divine administrative assembly whose decisions governed the earthly realm. The books that are opened are the record of deeds — cf. Revelation 20:12 for the final judgment scene that draws directly on this vision.
11: The little horn is slain because of the sound of its great words (min-qal milaya ravrevata di qarnah memmala). The boasting speech of the little horn — the “great things” (milaya ravrevata, boastful words) — is what triggers the beast's destruction. The same pattern as Nebuchadnezzar's boast in Daniel 4 (Daniel 4:30-31), and Belshazzar's feast in Daniel 5: human imperial self-assertion precipitates divine judgment. The body given to burning fire: a reversal of the furnace imagery in Daniel 3 — what tried to burn YHWH's servants is itself burned.
12: The dominion of the first three beasts is taken away, but their lives are prolonged for a season. This detail preserves the historical reality: the empires of Babylon, Persia, and Greece did not simply vanish when superseded — their cultural, linguistic, and administrative legacies persisted within the successor empire. The Aramaic language itself in Daniel is a legacy of Babylonian imperial administration adopted by Persia; Alexander deliberately adopted Persian administrative structures; Rome absorbed Greek culture. The beasts survive as cultural substrates even when their political dominion ends.
13: The Son of Man vision (Daniel 7:13-14) is the theological center of all of Daniel. The phrase kebar enash (one like a son of man / one like a human being) presents a deliberately ambiguous figure: human in appearance (kebar = like, as), coming from the heavenly realm with the clouds. The contrast with the beasts from the sea is the point: beasts rise from chaos (sea); this figure descends from the divine realm (clouds). He receives dominion from the Ancient of Days — authority given from above, not seized from below. The Second Temple Jewish texts (1 Enoch 37-71, 4 Ezra 13) develop the Son of Man as a preexistent heavenly figure who will appear at the end of history.
14: The dominion given to the Son of Man is universal, eternal, and indestructible: kol-amayya umevaya ulishanaya leh yiflehun (all peoples, nations, and languages shall serve him) and shaltehneh shaltan alam (his dominion is an everlasting dominion). Both characteristics explicitly contrast with the four beasts, whose dominions were temporary and destructible. The triadic nations-formula (peoples, nations, languages) has appeared throughout Daniel (Daniel 3:4; Daniel 4:1; Daniel 5:19; Daniel 6:25) as the measure of universal reach; here it is applied to the Son of Man's kingdom. The four empires' universal claims are fulfilled — and exceeded — in this figure.
15: Daniel's inner reaction: etkarev ruchi ana Daniel begov nidbeka (my spirit was troubled within me — within the sheath/body). The Aramaic nidneh (sheath/body) is unusual — it may be a poetic term for the body as the housing of the spirit. The visions themselves (khezwey rashe, visions of my head) disturb him even as he sees them. The prophetic experience of apocalyptic vision is frequently traumatic in the literature (cf. Isaiah 21:3-4; Habakkuk 3:16; Revelation 1:17). Understanding a vision of cosmic history does not eliminate the emotional weight of what is seen.
16: Daniel approaches one of those standing by (chad min-qamaya, one of those standing) — one of the heavenly beings in the court scene. The request is for yatsivata (certainty/reliable interpretation) about all of this. The heavenly interpreter figure is a feature of apocalyptic genre: the seer receives the vision but needs an angelic guide to interpret it (cf. Zechariah's horse-visions interpreted by an angel, Zechariah 1:8-19). In Daniel, Gabriel fills this role explicitly in Daniel 8-9; the unnamed interpreter here anticipates that function.
17: The interpretation begins at the level of the overall structure: ilein khayavata rabrevata arbaʿ malkheen yequmon min-arʿa (these four great beasts are four kings who shall arise from the earth). The “four kings” formula parallels the four-kingdom statue of Daniel 2 and connects the two interpretive frameworks. The word “arise from the earth” (min-arʿa) contrasts with the Son of Man who comes from heaven — earthly origin vs. heavenly origin is the fundamental contrast. Second Temple interpreters debated which empires the four kingdoms represented; the NT and most Christian interpreters settled on Babylon/Persia/Greece/Rome.
18: The saints of the Most High (qadishei ʿIllaya) will receive the kingdom and possess it forever. The identity of the qadishei ʿIllaya has been debated: (1) angelic beings (based on the language of “holy ones” used for angels); (2) the faithful Israelites; (3) the eschatological people of God inclusive of Gentiles. The NT applies the kingdom-inheritance to Christ's people: “the saints will judge the world” (1 Corinthians 6:2) and “you have made them a kingdom ... and they shall reign on the earth” (Revelation 5:10). The kingdom given to the Son of Man (Daniel 7:14) is also given to the saints who are united with him.
19: Daniel's focused inquiry about the fourth beast — specifically its iron teeth and bronze claws and its devouring nature — shows that the vision of the Son of Man and the saints receiving the kingdom has not eliminated his concern about the fourth beast's power and the suffering it will cause. The request for understanding about the beast reflects the pastoral concern of the apocalyptic genre: those who will suffer under the fourth empire need to understand not just the final outcome but the process of history they must endure.
20: The little horn is described more fully in the inquiry: v-ʿal qaran ileta di-sliqat (and concerning the other horn that came up) — the one before which three fell, with eyes and a mouth speaking great things, whose appearance was greater than its companions. The detail of khezva rab min-khabratah (its appearance was greater than its fellows) may suggest Antiochus IV's elaborate self-presentation — his coinage and propaganda were unusually developed compared to his Seleucid predecessors.
21: The little horn makes war with the saints (kazba im-qadishein v-yakhlah lehon, prevailing against them). This verse explicitly states that the little horn overcomes the saints — a sobering counter-narrative to simple triumphalism. Historical fulfillment: Antiochus IV's campaign of persecution (167-164 BCE) killed thousands of faithful Jews who refused to apostatize; the Maccabean martyrs described in 1 and 2 Maccabees and Hebrews 11:35-38. The NT reapplies the pattern to the church: “they were given authority to overcome” (Revelation 13:7, echoing Daniel 7:21). The suffering of the saints before their vindication is not a failure of YHWH's plan but part of it.
22: The Ancient of Days comes (umata ʿattiq yomaya), judgment is given to the saints, and the saints possess the kingdom. The sequence is precise: divine intervention → judgment for the saints → kingdom possession. The divine coming is the turning point — not human victory in battle but heavenly judicial intervention. This pattern shapes the NT's eschatology: the parousia of Christ is the moment of judgment and vindication for the suffering saints. The “coming of the Ancient of Days” functions in Daniel as the coming of Christ does in the NT — the cosmic event that ends the dominion of human empires.
23: The fourth beast is a fourth kingdom on earth, different from all other kingdoms, that will devour the whole earth and tread it down and break it to pieces. The universal scope — kol-arʿa (all the earth), not just a region — distinguishes the fourth beast from the preceding three. This universal reach matches the Roman empire's self-presentation and the NT's identification of Rome as the fourth kingdom. The language of devouring and breaking matches the iron that crushes in Daniel 2 — both describe a form of empire-power that destroys rather than absorbs (unlike the Persian and Ptolemaic policies of integration).
24: The ten horns are ten kings — and then a different king who subdues three. The succession of ten followed by an eleventh who uproots three has been applied historically to Antiochus IV (who came to power by ousting rivals) and eschatologically to a sequence of Roman emperors. The detail that the eleventh king is different from the earlier ones (v-hu yishna min-qadmaya) suggests a qualitative distinctiveness — not just political but perhaps religious or ideological (Antiochus IV's demand to be worshipped as a god was genuinely unusual in Seleucid practice).
25: The little horn speaks against the Most High, wears out the saints, and attempts to change times and law. The Aramaic yibla qadishei ʿIllaya (will wear out/afflict the saints) uses a verb meaning to exhaust by constant pressure — a sustained campaign of erosion rather than a single decisive blow. V-yisbar lehashnayyah zimnin v-dat (he will think to change the appointed times and the law): the assault is specifically on the calendar (the sacred moedim, feast-times) and the Torah. Antiochus IV explicitly abolished the Sabbath and Jewish festival calendar (1 Macc Daniel 1:41-50). The saints are given into his hand for “a time, times, and half a time” — three and a half years, matching 167-164 BCE historically.
26: The heavenly court sits in judgment and the little horn's dominion is taken away. The legal vocabulary returns: vdin yitiv (judgment shall be given), shaltehneh yehʿadon (his dominion shall be consumed), v-lehashmedah ulavadah ad-sofa (destroyed and brought to an end for good). The finality of the language distinguishes this judgment from the temporary removal of the first three beasts (Daniel 7:12). The little horn's end is total — not a temporary check but permanent destruction. Antiochus IV died of disease in 164 BCE, shortly after the Maccabean recapture of Jerusalem.
27: The climactic verse: the kingdom, the dominion, and the greatness of the kingdoms under the whole heaven are given to the people of the saints of the Most High. The phrase malkhutah v-shaltan v-rebot malkhevata takhot kol-shamayya (the kingdom and the dominion and the greatness of the kingdoms under all heaven) is a comprehensive summary: not just YHWH's heavenly kingdom but the earthly kingdoms as well. The saints receive what the four beasts claimed. The eternal kingdom: malkhuteh malkhut alam v-kol-shaltan leh yiflehun v-yishtemʿun (his kingdom is an everlasting kingdom and all dominions shall serve and obey him).
28: Daniel's personal response to the vision: saggi raʿyonay yivahalonani v-zivayi yishtannon ʿalai (my thoughts greatly alarmed me and my color changed). The vision's impact is physical as well as cognitive. Daniel keeps the matter in his heart (v-miltha b-libbi natrit, I kept the word/matter in my heart) — the Aramaic natr (to keep/guard) suggests careful preservation rather than passive forgetting. The sealing of the vision (Daniel 7:28 and cf. Daniel 8:26; Daniel 12:4) is a standard apocalyptic feature: visions given for the future are preserved in written form until the time of their fulfillment arrives.
